summary: Gruber announces he's moved his Google ads from a vertical sidebar position to a horizontal placement below article content. He acknowledges this defies conventional advertising wisdom by placing ads 'below the fold' and showing fewer ads at a time. His reasoning is that the vertical format was too visually distracting, and readers who actually read articles will encounter the ads naturally at the end. He bets that engaged readers are more valuable than casual visitors who never scroll.
key_points: Gruber relocated ads from vertical sidebar to horizontal placement below articles, prioritizing reading experience over ad visibility
He acknowledges this violates conventional advertising wisdom: fewer ads shown, positioned below the fold
His core argument is that readers who actually read content are the only ones likely to click ads anyway
The design change reflects his belief that reader experience should take priority over maximizing ad impressions
money_quotes: The men in suits would surely deem this a foolish idea.
I think the vertical format was overly distracting, and I couldn't stand subjecting you to it any longer.
I suspect that most of you tend to actually *read* these articles.
If you do, when you get to the end of an article, the ads will be there waiting for you.
Anyone who comes to this site (or any site for that matter), and then leaves without scrolling is unlikely to be clicking any ads, anyway.
key_insight: Optimizing for reader experience over conventional ad placement wisdom is the smarter long-term bet, because engaged readers who finish articles are the only audience worth monetizing.
